core_concept: Small Quantities: One to Four, More and Less
facts: When counting a group of up to four objects, the last number you say tells how many objects are in the group; for example, if you count 'one, two, three' and stop, there are three objects.
To compare two groups, you can match each object in one group to exactly one object in the other group; if every object has a partner and none are left over, the groups have the same number.
A group of four objects has more objects than a group of two objects because four comes after two when counting, and you can see that two is less than four.
misconceptions: A child may believe that a group of objects spread out over a larger area has more objects than a group of the same number of objects clustered together, because the spread-out group looks bigger.
